text: Using general retraction, the surgeon successfully reduces the incarcerated segment of the small bowel from the obturator hernia sac.
The surgeon inspects the bowel, noting some bruising but confirming the tissue appears viable.

The surgeon focuses on the hernia defect, with the superior pubic ramus visible as a highly reflective structure above the hernia. Additional retraction is applied to reduce the hernia sac, which is seen protruding through the obturator canal.

The laparoscopic view reveals bilateral indirect inguinal hernias, with the left hernia appearing wider and immediately visible upon entry into the abdomen, while the right hernia is identified shortly after the reduction of the obturator hernia.
The surgical team transitions to a total extraperitoneal approach, preparing to address the hernia repair from outside the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon displays a right-sided obturator hernia containing adipose tissue, visible within the surgical field.
The superior pubic ramus is visible as a reflective structure above the incarcerated fat, while the round ligament is seen crossing under the grasper towards the right side.
The surgeon reduces the incarcerated fat and uses blunt dissection with counter-traction to clear adipose and areolar tissue on the right side of the surgical field.
The surgeon defines the anatomy, bringing the superior pubic ramus into clearer view, highlighting its position relative to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon moves to the right of the round ligament, exposing the iliac artery and vein, which are visible in the surgical field.

The surgical field shows a cleared space over both inguinal and obturator hernias, providing adequate room for mesh placement. The anatomical structures are well-defined, with visible landmarks such as the superior pubic ramus and round ligament.
The surgeon focuses on the left side, identifying the original obturator hernia in relation to the superior pubic ramus, with the hernia visibly positioned near this anatomical landmark.
The surgeon grasps the hernia sac and applies general traction to reduce it, preparing the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon continues to define the anatomy on the left side, using blunt dissection and counter traction to easily separate the loosely adherent tissue, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon performs sweeping motions to further expose underlying anatomical structures, creating additional space for the planned mesh placement in the hernia repair procedure.
The iliac vessels are partially visible, running to the left of the round ligament, in the surgical field prepared for hernia repair.
The surgeon removes the camera and inserts a roll of polypropylene mesh into the surgical site, indicating adequate clearance has been achieved for mesh placement.
The surgeon examines the polypropylene mesh on the left side, ensuring it covers both the inguinal and obturator hernias.
The surgeon places a second piece of mesh on the right side, securing both the left and right mesh pieces to the underlying pubic bone using three visible tacks.
The preperitoneal balloon is slowly deflated, allowing the mesh to fall into place over the surgical site, visibly covering the intended area.
The camera is reinserted into the peritoneal cavity, revealing the mesh in place with the peritoneum intact and no mesh exposure visible.
The surgeon observes the fat contents of the hernia situated between the mesh and the peritoneum. A final examination of the bowel is conducted, confirming its viability.
